planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; Indian Hawthorn tolerates transplanting at any time of year in zone 9 but establishes best in spring or fall when root growth is active. Space 3–4 feet apart for a hedge or mass planting; 5–6 feet apart as a specimen. Avoid over-fertilizing — excessive nitrogen reduces flowering and increases disease susceptibility.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Indian Hawthorn (Raphiolepis indica) is arguably the most widely planted evergreen foundation shrub in Gulf Coast landscapes, carpeting suburban front yards from Houston to Jacksonville with its compact mounding form and clusters of fragrant pink or white apple-blossom flowers in February–April. The glossy, dark-evergreen leaves are leathery and handsome year-round; after bloom, small blue-black berries develop that attract mockingbirds and cedar waxwings. Dozens of named varieties exist in commerce ranging from 6-inch ground covers to 6-foot tall hedge plants: 'Olivia' (low-mounding, large white flowers), 'Eleanor Tabor' (pink flowers, dwarf), 'Clara' (white flowers, compact), 'Majestic Beauty' (upright, to 6 feet), and 'Indian Princess' (pink, very dwarf). Indian Hawthorn is exceptionally drought, heat, and salt spray tolerant — making it a top choice for coastal Gulf South gardens, highway medians, and HOA foundation beds that receive minimal irrigation or care. Entomosporium leaf spot is the primary disease in humid conditions; plant in locations with good air circulation and avoid wetting foliage with irrigation. Prune immediately after spring bloom to maintain shape without sacrificing the following year's flower buds.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Indian Hawthorn (Raphiolepis indica) landscape use
LSU AgCenter — Landscape Shrubs for Gulf Coast Louisiana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Indian Hawthorn for Florida Landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Raphiolepis indica

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown plants in late winter or early spring; seeds ripen in summer inside distinctive woody pods and germinate the following spring with minimal stratification. Mexican Buckeye grows slowly in the first year but accelerates once established. In zone 9, plant in a partially shaded understory position or along a woodland edge where it receives morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline-tolerant soil in partial to full shade; Mexican Buckeye (Ungnadia speciosa) is a native multi-stemmed large shrub or small tree indigenous to the limestone hills and cedar brakes of central and west Texas, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, where it thrives in the dry shade beneath live oak canopies on thin, rocky, alkaline soils. The cloud of bright magenta-pink flowers that appears before the leaves in February–March is startling and beautiful — one of the earliest spring bloomers in the Gulf South native plant palette. The leathery, pinnately compound leaves (resembling the Ohio Buckeye's leaves) turn clear yellow in fall. The distinctive round, woody seed pods split open to reveal 1–3 shiny, mahogany-brown seeds resembling horse chestnuts — they are toxic (containing saponins and cytisine) and should not be eaten. Mexican Buckeye is extremely drought and alkaline-soil tolerant once established; it thrives where soils are too rocky and poor for most other ornamentals. The dense growth provides excellent cover and nesting habitat for mockingbirds and other cavity nesters. A superb native choice for dry shade situations in limestone-soil zone 7–10 gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Ungnadia speciosa (Mexican Buckeye)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Trees of Texas: Mexican Buckeye
Native Plant Society of Texas — Ungnadia speciosa profile
University of Texas at Austin Biodiversity Center — Plant Resources of the Southwest

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost; in zone 9 sow March–May for summer and fall display. Love-in-a-Puff self-seeds prolifically in zone 9–10 and will return each year from dropped seeds without replanting. It is a native vine in the Gulf South and will naturalize in moist, sunny garden edges.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in moist, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; nick the seed coat or soak seeds overnight in warm water before planting to improve germination. Love-in-a-Puff (Cardiospermum halicacabum) is a delightful lightweight twining annual vine native throughout the tropics and subtropics including the Gulf Coast, where it scrambles through shrubs and fence wire producing a continuous display of tiny white flowers and its signature inflated, papery, three-winged seed pods that look exactly like miniature balloons or Japanese paper lanterns — the pods are responsible for the plant's whimsical common name. Inside each round green pod are three round, black seeds, each with a perfect white heart-shaped marking where they were attached to the pod wall — the 'heart seed' of the scientific name Cardiospermum. The vine is lightweight and delicate, climbing trellises, fences, and other plants to 8–10 feet by twining tendrils, never heavy or smothering. In zone 9–10, it naturalizes freely from dropped seeds. The leaves and seeds are used medicinally in Ayurvedic medicine for joint inflammation; research has identified anti-inflammatory compounds in the plant. An excellent, low-care vine for adding airy texture and charming seed pods to Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cardiospermum halicacabum (Love-in-a-Puff)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Cardiospermum halicacabum
USDA Plants Database — Cardiospermum halicacabum
Tropic Zone Plants Reference Database — Balloon Vine ethnobotanical uses

common_name: Texas Yellow Star (Texas Star Daisy, Lindheimera)

planting.note: In zone 9, direct sow in fall (October–November) for late winter and spring bloom; in zone 7–8 sow in early spring. Lindheimera texana is endemic to limestone hills of central Texas and needs excellent drainage — avoid clay soils or supplement with coarse sand and gravel. Self-sows readily in zone 9 gardens and returns year after year with no replanting once established.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface or barely covered with fine grit in very well-drained, lean, alkaline-tolerant soil in full sun; Texas Yellow Star does not require rich soil — in fact, excessive fertility produces weak, floppy stems with few flowers. Lindheimera texana is a delightful annual wildflower endemic to the limestone Hill Country of central Texas and is one of the few monotypic genera in the Asteraceae — the only species in its genus, named for Ferdinand Jacob Lindheimer, the pioneering 19th-century Texas botanist. The cheerful, bright-yellow, five-rayed daisy flowers cover the compact plants from March through June in Texas, and self-seeding plants bloom into summer wherever drainage is excellent. Flowers have a slightly unusual structure: the ray florets are not ligulate but have distinctly notched petals that give them a whimsical, multi-pointed star appearance. An excellent companion in wildflower meadows alongside bluebonnets, wine cups, and Indian paintbrush in zone 8–9 limestone garden settings. Once self-seeding populations establish in a well-drained spot, they return reliably each year without replanting. Deer resistant.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Lindheimera texana (Texas Yellow Star)
Native Plant Society of Texas — Lindheimera texana
USDA Plants Database — Lindheimera texana
Texas Wildflower Days — Annual wildflowers for Hill Country

planting.note: Plant transplants in spring after last frost in zone 9; established plants are drought-tolerant and semi-evergreen. Blackfoot Daisy requires sharp drainage — it will rot in heavy clay or wet soils. Add coarse gravel or decomposed granite to heavy soils before planting. In zone 9, plants may go semi-dormant in the hottest summer weeks and resume blooming as temperatures cool.

directions: Plant in extremely well-drained, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; excellent drainage is non-negotiable — Blackfoot Daisy (Melampodium leucanthum) will not survive in clay soils that stay wet after rain. Melampodium leucanthum is a small, mounding native perennial that blooms nearly year-round in zone 8–10, covering itself with perfectly formed white daisies with yellow centers, each approximately 1 inch across. It is native to the semi-arid limestone hills of Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, where it grows in thin, rocky, alkaline soils in full sun with minimal rainfall. In zone 9 gardens, Blackfoot Daisy is an exceptional xeriscape and rock garden plant that requires no supplemental irrigation once established and no deadheading — spent flowers simply fall cleanly from the plant. The honey-scented flowers attract small native bees, skippers, and butterflies throughout the growing season. Plants are remarkably long-lived in the right conditions, forming expanding mounds of fine-textured gray-green foliage that are attractive even when not in bloom. Trim back hard in late summer to encourage fresh growth and extended fall blooming. Note: the annual Melampodium paludosum (Medallion Flower) is a distinct tropical species from the same genus but with different cultural requirements.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Melampodium leucanthum
Native Plant Society of Texas — Blackfoot Daisy
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Blackfoot Daisy (Melampodium leucanthum)
USDA Plants Database — Melampodium leucanthum
